---
title: "Why Matching Presenter Script"
subtitle: "A fuller talk track for the concept deck"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    code-links:
      - text: Why We Do Matching deck
        href: why_matching.html
      - text: Why Matching Speaking Notes
        href: why_matching_notes.html
      - text: Main report
        href: ../articles/matching_methods_report.html
execute:
  echo: false
  eval: false
---

## How To Use This Script

Use this page when you want a fuller spoken version of the deck rather than cue cards.

- Read it once or twice to get the flow into your ear.
- Trim examples or transitions if you need to get under `20` minutes.
- Keep the shorter speaking notes open instead if you want prompts rather than a near-verbatim script.

## Slide-By-Slide Script

### Title Slide

Today I want to explain why matching exists before we talk about any specific matching algorithm. The main point is that matching is a response to a causal-design problem. It is not a magic software move and it is not a badge that makes observational data behave like an experiment.

### Session Promise

This deck starts from the missing counterfactual, then moves through confounding, and only then arrives at matching. That order matters. If we start with software, matching can look like a technical trick. If we start with causal inference, matching looks like what it really is: an attempt to build a more credible comparison group.

### 20-Minute Arc

The structure is simple. First, I will explain the basic counterfactual problem and why causal inference always needs a comparison group. Second, I will show why randomization is the clearest benchmark and why observational comparisons usually fail. Third, I will explain what matching is trying to repair, and then I will end with the assumptions and limits that still remain.

### Start With The Fundamental Problem

For any unit, there are two potential outcomes: one if the unit receives treatment and one if it does not. The problem is that we only ever observe one of those outcomes for each unit. The other one is missing. That missing value is the counterfactual, and causal inference is the task of finding a credible stand-in for it.

### Why We Need A Comparison Group

That is why every causal design is, at bottom, a comparison-group problem. We observe what happened under the treatment state the unit actually received, but we do not observe what would have happened under the alternative state. So the design task is always to build or find a comparison group that is credible enough to stand in for that missing outcome.

### Best Benchmark: Randomization

The cleanest benchmark is randomization. If treatment is randomized, then treated and untreated groups are comparable in expectation before treatment begins. That is why randomized controlled trials are the clearest benchmark for causal comparison quality. But they are still a benchmark, not magic: attrition, noncompliance, and external-validity problems can still matter.

### Why Matching Enters The Story

Matching only enters because many important policy and programme questions are not randomized. Treatment has already happened, and the comparison group is not automatically credible. In those settings, matching is a second-best design repair. The job is to recover the best comparison the data can still defend when a stronger design is not available.

### Why Raw Comparisons Mislead

The core problem in observational data is that treatment is usually related to prior characteristics. People who get treated may differ from controls before treatment starts, and those same differences may also affect the outcome. That means the raw treated-control gap is usually mixing together the effect of treatment and the effect of pre-existing differences.

### The Confounder Problem

That mixture is what we call confounding. A confounder is a common cause of treatment and outcome. Once that common-cause path exists, the observed association between treatment and outcome is no longer a clean causal effect. Association and causation separate because part of the relationship is being driven by selection into treatment.

### Backdoor Path

This slide shows the basic structure. `Prior need` affects who gets treatment, and it also affects the outcome. So if I compare treated and untreated units without addressing `Prior need`, I am not just comparing treatment states. I am also comparing units that entered the analysis with different baseline levels of need. That is the backdoor problem matching is trying to repair.

### What This Means For Design

Once we see that structure, the design requirement becomes clearer. If treated and untreated units differ on `Prior need`, then the raw outcome gap is confounded. Part of the gap reflects pre-treatment selection and not only treatment. The direction of the bias depends on how `Prior need` affects the outcome, which is why it is safer to say the raw gap is confounded than to claim a fixed bias direction.

### Block The Backdoor

The design goal is to block that backdoor path by conditioning on the right pre-treatment variables. Regression can do this, matching can do this, subclassification can do this, and weighting can do this. The methods differ in implementation, but the causal target is the same: compare treated and untreated units after the confounding path has been addressed.

### Matching In One Sentence

So the shortest defensible definition of matching is this: matching compares treated units with untreated units that are similar on relevant observed pre-treatment covariates. The important words there are `relevant`, `observed`, and `pre-treatment`. Matching is a design tool for reducing observed confounding. It is not a guarantee that all confounding has disappeared.

### Why Match?

The strongest practical reason to match is not just that imbalance looks ugly. It is that imbalance creates model dependence. If treated and control units are far apart in covariate space, the estimate depends heavily on modeling choices, functional form, and extrapolation. Matching helps by removing or down-weighting the least credible comparisons before the model summarizes the data.

### Why Matching Appeals In Practice

That is why matching is attractive in applied work. It makes the comparison group more visible, it reduces hidden extrapolation, and it forces design diagnostics into the open. Instead of pretending a coefficient alone settles the question, matching makes the analyst show who is actually being compared to whom and what that costs in terms of retention or weights.

### Design In Two Steps

The cleanest way to think about matching is as a two-step workflow. First, preprocess the design: define the estimand, choose covariates from the assignment story, inspect imbalance and overlap, and then adjust the design. Second, estimate the effect on the redesigned sample or weighting scheme. That split helps keep design decisions separate from outcome-model fishing.

### From Subclassification To Matching

Subclassification is the clearest starting point for the logic. If you can compare treated and untreated units within the same covariate-defined strata, the causal story is intuitive. Exact matching is the strict version of that rule. Approximate matching methods are ways of relaxing exact equality when the data become too sparse, but the underlying idea stays the same: compare like with like.

### The Curse Of Dimensionality

The difficulty is that once many covariates matter at once, exact strata multiply very quickly. Cells become tiny or empty, and overlap disappears for some parts of the treated group. That is why matching methods exist in the first place. The causal logic is simple, but realistic data rarely support exact comparisons everywhere we would like them to.

### Good Controls, Not All Controls

At that point, variable choice becomes central. The question is not simply which baseline variables are available. The real question is which variables help block confounding without opening new problems. Matching should target plausible pre-treatment common causes. It should not become a kitchen-sink exercise that throws in every measured variable without a causal story.

### Collider Warning

This is where bad controls matter. A collider is not a confounder. If two variables both cause a third variable and we condition on that third variable, we can create a spurious relationship that did not exist before. So even a pre-treatment variable is not automatically safe just because it was measured early. Causal reasoning has to come before software.

### What Matching Is Buying

What matching buys us is design improvement, not proof. It can reduce observed baseline imbalance, make overlap problems visible, and force the analyst to report design costs more plainly. Those are real gains. But they are gains in the quality and transparency of the comparison, not automatic identification guarantees.

### Matching And Weighting Are Siblings

It is also helpful to see matching and weighting as siblings. Matching changes who remains in the sample. Weighting changes how much each unit counts in the comparison. Both are trying to solve the same underlying problem, and many matching estimators can be understood as weighted averages rather than as mysterious black-box procedures.

### One Logic, Several Implementations

That is why regression, matching, stratification, and weighting belong in the same conceptual family. They are different implementations of the same larger idea: compare treated and untreated units after blocking backdoor paths with observed covariates. Once you understand that shared logic, the methods menu becomes much easier to navigate.

### Matching Is Still About Assumptions

None of this removes the core assumptions. Matching still relies on no important unmeasured confounders, usable overlap, actual balance on observed covariates after adjustment, and a stable estimand that still answers the original question. Matching can improve design, but it cannot make these assumptions disappear.

### What Matching Cannot Do

That means matching has hard limits. It cannot fix omitted confounding, it cannot rescue units for which no comparable controls exist, and it cannot justify bad controls. Most importantly, it cannot convert observational data into randomized data. If the design problem is too severe, matching does not erase that fact.

### When Not To Match

So we should not default to matching just because it is available in software. If a stronger design such as a randomized experiment, `RDD`, or credible `DiD` is available, use that instead. And if overlap is so weak that only severe pruning or extreme weights make the design work, that is a substantive warning about what the data can support.

### Why We Still Do It

Even with those warnings, we still do matching because many important questions are observational and policy-relevant. Regression alone can hide the comparison problem rather than expose it. Matching makes the counterfactual argument more visible and can reduce dependence on pure model form, even when it cannot eliminate all uncertainty.

### Practical Standard

So the practical standard is straightforward. Define the estimand first. Choose covariates from the assignment story. Inspect imbalance and overlap. Match or weight as a design step. Estimate only after the adjusted design is credible enough to defend, and then follow that with uncertainty checks and sensitivity checks rather than treating the estimate as self-validating.

### What The Lab Focuses On

In the lab, we narrow the menu on purpose. We focus on exact matching as the transparency benchmark, coarsened exact matching as the main approximate matching method, and entropy balancing as the weighting comparison. We do not center nearest-neighbor propensity-score matching or the full advanced `MatchIt` method menu on the first pass. That narrower scope keeps the design rule visible, and people who want the broader menu can use the official [MatchIt documentation](https://kosukeimai.github.io/MatchIt/).

### Closing Line

The line I want to leave in the room is simple: we do matching to reduce observed confounding by building a more credible comparison group, while staying honest that causality still depends on assumptions the data cannot prove. If the audience remembers that sentence, the deck has done its job.
